ABSTRACT 
 The need for improving flexibility, optimality, and transparency of industry processes 
increases since the customer demands are highly diverse and full of uncertainty. Cyber -
Physical System (CPS) technol

ogy now is being developed to meet those requirements. It can 
be achieved through Digital Twin (DT). Unfortunately, the application of DT is still limited 
in practice nowadays, and no stan dard way to achieve integration. The concept of an Asset 
Administration Shell (AAS) proposed by Plattform Industrie 4.0 arose as a promising concept 
to realize DT  in standard manner . However, the gra

nularity of AAS remains undefined and 
still under develop ment. A literature study shows that most of studies only reached static 
digital twin and only considered a few specific assets  especially unmovable assets. Dynamic 
digital twin is only supported by Virtual Reality (VR

) that cannot be used for optimization as 
well as direct controlling. Therefore, this research  intends to contribute to DT development 
by proposing 3D computer simulation technology as dynamic digital twin by following 
Reference Architecture Model Industrie 4.0 (RAMI 4.0) based on AAS Framework . The 
proposed concept enables DT to do dynamic monitoring, optimization , and direct controlling. 
As a use case, a smart warehouse in Taiwan is used and assets considered include Automated 
Guided Vehicles (AGVs), Operator, Conveyor, Forklift, and Storage R ack. This research 
consists of 4 stages. First, AAS Variables Definition, identifying the assets and the

data that 
want to be exchanged via AAS, defining AAS capability and AAS structure. Second, AAS 
Simulation Modeling with Flexsim software is established . Third, AAS System Integration 
consists of designing communication networks and information modeling. And last, 
demonstrating 2 main advantages of using simulation which are simulation -based 
optimization for AGV capacity planning and creating dynamic dash board. To support 
communication between assets, OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) and Automation 
Markup Language (AutomationML) are determined. As a result of demonstration, 
simulation

Dynamic dashboard is also created to ease real-time monitoring process. Later 
on, data from dashboard can be exported to database and predictive analytics can be done. 
Based on these capabilities, industry 4.0 scenarios can be integ rated systematically and 
comprehensi

vely.  
 
Keywords : Asset Administration Shell, Digital Twin, Simulation, Optimization

INTRODUCTION  
1.1. Background 
Manufacturing Digitalization is the main important purpose in this fourth industrial 
revolution or also termed as Industry 4.0 (I4.0) because of high diversity in customer demands, 
high level of uncertainty , and fast-changing market conditions [1]. This forces  the production 
mode in industry to shift from mass produc

tion to mass customization  [2]. However, these 
necessities lead to increase production system complexity .  
Digitalization is also the way to  improve flexibility, adaptability, optimality, and 
transparency of industry processes. Cyber -Physical System (CPS) technology right now is 
bei

ng developed to meet thos e requirements. It is achieved by building a digital asset that is 
real-time connected with its physical asset and enabling bidirectional information exchange 
between them, so -called Digital Twin  [3]. It is called ‘Twin’ because it is like mirroring or 
twinning of systems between what existed in real sp ace to what existed in virtual space. 
To implement Digital Twin, the Internet is the basic technology to make it happen.

At 
the same time, Internet has developed rapidly and seems to be a necessity for everyone in the 
world nowadays. The advanced developme nt of technology makes internet at a modest cost  
and borderless. Even in 2019, the number of internet users worldwide stood at 4.13 billion 
which means that more than half of the global population is currently connected to the inter

net 
[4]. As the internet evolves, Industry 4.0  occurred to aim at transforming traditional industry  
into intelligent ones  through the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) . For Industry 4.0, the 
computer is not the central technology, but t

he Internet [5]. Internet has important role to ensure 
interope rability of connected components, equipment, and products in industry. It also supports 
all digitization activities in industry and makes  smart factories to be reality [6]. 
There are a lot of research groups in some countries that start to realize digital twin in 
smart factory. Europe first launched factories of the future program (2008-2020 with investing 
2.35 Billion Euro) then Singapore launched Future of Manufacturing (FoM) with investment 
500M SD in 2014  [7], and Germany invested 200  Million Euro in 2012 launching

Plattform 
Industrie 4.0  [8].  As a result, the concept of an Asset Administration Shell (AAS) proposed by 
Plattform Industrie 4.0 is one of the most promising approach es to realize digital twin in  the 
context of Industry 4.0 (I4.0) Component  [9], in line with the standardized refere nce model 
called Reference Architecture Model for Industry 4.0 (RAMI 4.0) [5]. 

2 
 
AAS basically consists of two terms w

hich are ‘Asset’ and ‘Administration Shell’. The 
Asset refers to the physical things e.  g. machine s & materials, and the administration shel l 
refers to the digital thing that stores information about the asset over its entire lifecycle. In 
smart manufacturing, each physical thing or asset will have its own administration shell, and 
each administration shell ca

n communicate with the other administration shells. However, until 
today the granularity of AAS remains undefined and still under development, with very few 
understanding how to employ an AAS in practice [10]. Therefore , AAS is very cha llenging 
topic and still has big research opportuni

ties . 
Previous studies  have been tried to implement AAS. [11] and [12] proposed  AAS for 
robot arm  and conveyor  as assets and proposed  Web User Interface (UI)  as its static digital 
twin. [13] and [14] designed AAS for workers so-called Human Administration Shell (HAS) 
and use Human -Machine Interface (HMI) as its static digital twin. [15] tried to implement 
digital twin in air quality sensor based on Things Description (TD) and proposed Docker Image 
as its static digital twin. And [7] tried to implement AAS in wider a

rea which is assembly 
process and created HMI as its static digital twin. 
Based on previous studies, AAS application s in realizing DT are still limited in 
visualization aspect which only show static model rather than dynamic model. Therefore, this 
research  will develop simulation -based digital t

win using Flexsim software based on AAS 
framework in order to create dynamic DT. Besides that, previous studies only considered a few 
specific  assets and movable assets . However, practically there will be a lot of assets that need 
to be considered  and connected. In this research, a smart warehouse is used as research object, 
it will widen the assets considered in AAS framework. Moreover, previous state-of-the-art 
analysis about DT stated that even tough previous researches were done, but there are still lack 
of DT that can reflect optimization [16]. By using proposed concept , optimization can be easily 
done and it will be demonstrated in this research for optimizing AGV capacity planning and 
designing dynamic dashboard  to enhance monitoring process . This research also intends to 
participate in industry 4.0 development . 
 
1.2. Problem Formulation 
Based on the bac kground above, the problem formulations in this research are: 
1. What is the concept for simulation -based digital twin with Flexsim software in smart 
warehouse based on AAS framework?  

3 
 
2. What is the design of simulation model and system integration architecture between 
physical world and digital world for smart warehouse?  
3. What are the main advantages of using simulation for digital twin based on AAS 
framework?  
 
1.3. Research Objectives 
The purposes of  this research are: 
1. Develop new concept for simulation-based digital twin with Flexsim software in smart 
warehouse based on AAS framework.  
2. Provide simulation model and  system integration architecture between physical world 
and digital world. 
3. Demonstrate simulation -based multi-objective optimization for AGV capacity planning 
and designing dynamic dashboard as main advantages of simulation.  
 
1.4. Research Benefits 
Based on the purpose of the research, this research  is developed to give the benefit and 
contribution as follows:  
1. Bring new concept by utilizing simulation especially Flexsim software for digital twin 
development based on AAS framework.  This proposed concept will enable digital twin 
into dynamic and having optimization capability.  
2. This research widens the assets considered in DT and very important in terms of 
proposing submodels  in AAS. Not only unmovable assets but also movable assets . 
3. Propose preliminary study of system integration for smart warehouse. 
 
1.5. Research Limitations 
There are some limitations in this research as follows: 
1. This research  only reaches concept ual development, simulation modeling, and system 
integration, not until implementation.  
2. Only 5 main assets considered  in smart warehouse which are Automated Guided 
Vehicle (AGV), Operator, Conveyor, Forklift, and Storage Rack. 
3. There is no economic analysis considered. This research will focus in technical aspects. 

4 
 
1.6. Organization of Thesis 
The framework of this research is explained in the following chapters. Chapter 1 is the 
background, objectives, limitations, and organization of the thesis. Chapter 2 presents the 
inductive study and deductive study as well as state-of-the-art of research related to AAS 
application and novelty of this research. Chapter 3 illustrates the research methods used to 
achieve research objectives. It includes 4 main stages which are AAS Variables Definition, 
AAS Simulation Modeling, AAS System Integration, and Demo nstrations. Chapter 4 presents 
the result of each stage from chapter 3. Chapter 5 will wrap up the conclusion from all chapters 
and also propose future research. Figure 1.1 below illustrates the organization of this thesis.

2. CHAPTER 2 
LITERATURE REVIEW 
This chapter is divided into three parts. The first two parts will show review result that 
is originated from the literature s, namely deductive study and inductive study. Deductive study 
is about reviewing literatures  obtained from books, reports, journals, and proceedings related 
to basic theory used in this research . Meanwhile, the inductive study is about reviewing 20 
previous researches obtained from journals and proceedings only that contains similar research 
to this research  which is AAS-based Digital Twin. The last part will underline  the difference 
and the novelty of this research compared with the others.  
 
2.1. Deductive Review 
2.1.1 Industry 4.0 
The fou rth industrial revolution, also named Industry 4.0 (I4.0), will digitalize 
manufacturing operations and decentralize them from the shop floor to the office and over 
whole enterprise networks  [1]. The main objective of Industry 4.0 is satisfying individual 
customer needs which influences areas like order management, manufacturing operations, 
delivery to customers [17]. The four main drivers of Industry 4.0 are the Industrial Internet of 
Things (IIoT), Cloud-based Manufacturing, Internet of Things (IoT), and Smart Manufacturing 
which helps in transforming manufacturing processes into fully digital and intelligent processes 
[18].  
Another stud y also proposes 9 pil lars of I4.0 which are Big Data & Analytics, 
Autonomous Robots, Simulation, System Integration, The Industrial Internet of Things, Cyber 
security & Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS), The Cloud, Additive Manufacturing, and 
Augmented Reality  [17]. But the challenge is how to integrate those t echnology into fully 
integrated and provides standard way to implement.  
Plattform Industrie 4.0 (2016) [19] proposed 9 application scenarios of industry 4.0 to 
ease understanding of implementation in practice:  
1. OCP – Order-Controlled Production: Autonomous and automated interconnection of 
production.  
2. AF – Adaptable Factory: Plug & Produce – adaptable manufacturing configurations 
within a factory for quickly changing production capacities and capabilities.  

6 
 
3. SAL – Self-organising Adaptive Logistics: Increasing flexibility and reaction time of 
industrial and  logistics systems in increasingly volatile and adaptable  production 
environments.  
4. VBS – Value-Based Services: Process and status data from production and product use 
sources are the raw materials for future business models and  services.  
5. TAP – Transparency and Adaptability of delivered Products: Automatic collection of 
use-based data from delivered products for optimising business processes, for new 
busines s models and for dynamic adaptation of product features.  
6. OSP – Operator Support in Production: Human -technology-human interaction for 
assisting humans as actors in the manufacturing process.  
7. SP2 – Smart Product Development for Smart Production: Virtual p roducts allow for 
new types of teamwork in engineering processes and automation of engineering 
activities.  
8. IPD – Innovative Product Development: Sufficiently integrating all relevant 
stakeholders in the engineering process and using a suitable methodical approach helps 
to develop the product in the right way.  
9. CRE – Circular Economy: High -grade recycling of materials from manufacturing 
during the entire life cycle of a product.  
 
2.1.2 Reference Architecture Model for Industry 4.0 (RAMI 4.0)  
In discussing Industr y 4.0, there is a framework that approaches the issue of Industry 
4.0 in a structured manner, namely Reference Architecture Model for Industry 4.0 (RAMI 4.0) 
proposed by German Plattform Industrie 4.0 shown in Figure 2.1 [5]. Actually, there are also 
other framework s with the same purpose like Industrial Internet Reference Architecture (IIRA) 
proposed  by IIC from US  [20], IBM Industry 4.0 Architecture  proposed by IBM [21], and 
NIST Smart Manufacturing  [22].  
Compared to others, RAMI 4.0 gives more comprehensive  information and more 
relevant to be implemented in industry especially for smart manufacturing [21]. RAMI 4.0 
provides an orientation for plotting the requirements of sectors together with standards in order 
to define and further develop Industry 4.0 [23]. It is implemented in Industry 4.0 aspects for 
smart manufacturing which combines all elements and IT components [24].  
 

7 
 
Since the main challenge of industry 4.0 is interoperability, it is also shown in RAMI 
4.0 from architectural layers which are divided into 2 aspects, namely physical aspects  and 
digital aspects. The physical thing is called an asset, while the digital aspect starts from 
communication layers until business layers as shown in Figure 2.1. To change the physical 
thing to the digital, there is integration layer between asset and c ommunication layers that 
applies the Digital Twin (DT) concept.  
 
Figure 2. 1 Reference Architecture Model for Industry 4.0  
(Source: [5]) 
 
2.1.3 Digital Twin 
The concept of the Digital Twin (DT) came from the presentation in 2002 entitled 
“Conceptual Ideal for Product Lifecycle Management (PLM)” by Dr. Grieves [25]. It is called 
‘Twin’ because it is like mirroring or twinning of systems between what existed in real space 
to what existed in virtual space and vice versa. But at that time, internet was still expensive and 
limited. Since DT uses internet as the basic technology so that the development of DT did not 
appear. But today, internet becomes cheaper and borderless so that many industries have 
implemented DT to enhance interoperability across value stream of an asset like shown in 
Figure 2.2 [26]. 
The concept of DT will be diffe rent with the other modeling concept even others show 
like mirroring the physical system. For example, Computer Aided Manufacturing (CAM) is 
also mirroring the physical system but it does not have capability to connect physical system 
with digital system w ith bi-directional. This is the main difference of DT concept compared 
with others. In DT, digital system will be connected in real-time and have capability to control 
physical system. However, CAM or others concept can also be applied as part of DT.  


8 
 
 
Figure 2. 2 Development of D igital Twin in 2015 
(Source: [26]) 
Digital twin (DT) is known as the basic concept to support integration in Industry 4.0 
and refers to virtual models of an asset that are fully integrated with a real thing/asset [27].  In 
DT, digital asset and physical asset are connected by internet with semantic relationships. It 
can be used for virtual testing, analyzing collected data, and predicting future behavior o f 
physical things before applying it to real things [15]. Since DT has so many advantages, DT 
can be practically applied in almost every industry like shown in Table 2.1.

Besides that, DT also can solve information silos problems as shown in Figure 2.3. 
Traditionally, the information about an asset is usually scattered across multiple information 
sources, which are maintained by different organizations. It will result in du plicated, 
inconsistent, and missing information. With digital twin, it can collect data centrally for every 
entity and then makes that information available to different areas of the business for their 
specific applications through integration interfaces, such as Application Programming 
Interfaces (APIs ) [29]. This improves decision -making through a shared unde rstanding and 
reduces the overall lifecycle cost of operating and maintaining a plant . 
 
 
Figure 2. 3 Digital Twin to Solve Information Silos Problem  
(Source: [29]) 
 
2.1.4 Asset Administration Shell 
One of the approaches to realize Digital Twin in manufacturing industry is called Asset 
Administration Shell (AAS) [29]. AAS basically consists of two terms which are ‘Asset’ and 
‘Administration Shel l’. The asset refers to the physical things e.g. machine, product, & 
materials, and the administration shell refers to the digital thing that stores information about 
the asset over its entire lifecycle. The AAS can be understood as  software entity providi ng 
asset’s information to the external by means of a standardized Application Programming 
Interface (API). In smart manufacturing, each physical thing or asset has its own administration 
shell, and each administration shell can connect with the other admin istration shell as shown 
in Figure 2.4 [29]. 


10 
 
 
Figure 2. 4 Concept of Asset Administration Shell  
(Source: [29]) 
 
The structure of an AAS can be isolated into a digital factory (DF) header and body like 
shown  in Figure 2.5 [11]. The concept of the DF system has standardized in International 
Electrotechnical Commission (IEC) standard 62832 [10]. The DF header highlig hts a list of 
I4.0-compliant properties, such as identification of the physical asset and of the AAS [30]. This 
must be included to clearly assign the unique identity of the I4.0 Component. This property list 
is termed a manifest, characterized as “an externally accessible defined set of meta-information, 
which provides information about the functional and non -functional properties of the I4.0 
Component” [31]. The DF body contains a component manager that  is mindful ly responsible 
for the management of and access to services in individual submodels. Each submodel consists 
of hierarchically well -organized properties linked to various data or functions. 


11 
 
 
Figure 2. 5 Structure of AAS 
(Source: [30]) 
By using AAS, interoperability can  be enhance d not only within company, but also 
between companies in the same supply chain network such as supplier and manufacturer. AAS 
also can be integrated with the other pillars in industry 4.0 such as Big Data since the data from 
the asset will be recorded over the lifecycle of asset (volume) for every second (veracity) and 
for every specification of asset (variety), Cloud Computing since the data will be stored and 
accessed from cloud, Simulation for doing trial and error based on scenario designed, and IoT 
that is embedded in asset s.  
2.1.5 AutomationML 
AutomationML or Automation Markup Language or AML is pointed at accomplishing 
standardized and efficient data exchange during the engineering processes of a manufacturing 
system, which can reduce time and cost  [12]. The main idea is, instead of developing  a new 
data format for that purpose, AutomationML adapts, extends, and merges already existing 
standardized data formats in an appropriate way . AML has been standardized in IEC 62714. 
AML is an open and neutral XML -based data exchange format that can be ut ilized for 
product, process, and resource descriptions within the production -system engineering domain 
[32]. The basic structure of AutomationML is shown in Figure 2.6 [33]. The core of 
AutomationML is the top-level data format Computer Aided Engineering Exchange (CAEX), 
which connects distinctive engineering data formats, such as Collaborative Design Activity 
(COLLADA) and PLCopen XML [34]. Moreover, AutomationML has an inherent distributed 
architecture established on the concept of the object -oriented method, as does CAEX. 


12 
 
 
Figure 2. 6 Structure of AML 
(Source: [33]) 
2.1.6 OPC UA 
Open Platform Communications Unified Architecture (OPC UA)  technology 
accomplishes message -based communication between clients and servers on different types of 
networks to facilitate various leveled industrial system information exchange, such as in 
connection to field devices, control systems, manufacturing execution systems , and enterprise 
resource planning systems  [35]. OPC UA has been standardized in IEC 62541. 
OPC UA follows client -server system. The OPC UA client uses the client application 
program interface (API) to send/receive OPC UA service request/response messages (Req/Rsp 
Msg) to/from the OPC UA server. Here, service is designed to discover the  OPC UA servers 
that are available in a particular system environment. Furthermore, the client API was 
developed to act as an internal interface to set the client application code apart from the client 
communication stack. The role of the communication sta ck is to support data transportation 
between the client and server, which converts the client API calls to messages and sends them 
through the underlying communication entity to the OPC UA server [36]. In OPC UA Server, 
it has an “address space” that is composed of sets of nodes, representing objects, variables, 
methods, and types of OPC UA metadata  [12]. Figure 2.7 shows the OPC UA client/server 
architecture  [37]. It figures the major elements of a typical OPC UA client and server as well 
as the communication process used to interact with each other.  


13 
 
 
Figure 2. 7 Structure of OPC UA 
(Source: [37]) 
In RAMI 4.0 perspective, OPC UA is located in the communication layer and serves as 
an interface for AAS communication. The advantage of OPC UA compared to Fieldbus and 
others is that OPC UA provides a unique information model composed of various node objects, 
properties, and references  [10]. This is the reason why the AML model can be integrated into 
the OPC UA because the AML metadata can be mapped to the OPC UA nodes in a standardized 
manner  [38]. 
 
2.1.7 Discrete-Event Simulation and Simulation Optimization 
Discrete-Event Simulation (DES) quantitatively represents the real world, simulates its 
dynamics on an event -by-event under uncertainty, and generates detailed performance report 
based on simulation. It has become one of the mainstream computer -aided decision-making 
tools due to accessibility of effective computers  [39]. Discrete event simulation (DES) is also 
a flexible modeling method cha racterized by the ability to represent complex behavior within, 
and interactions between individuals, populations, and their environments [40]. It can mimic 
operations that occur in a system with the help of advanced computer and software based on 
some assumptions so that the system can be studied scientifically  [39]. 


14 
 
In Digital Twin perspective, advanced simulation must be able to visualize the real 
system as similar as possible. With the help of advanced simu lation software, now it can be 
achieved to use simulation as part of digital twin. Another thing that should be considered about 
simulation is it has a lot of advantages that support Industry 4.0 such as capability to do What-
If analysis , simulation optimization, accurate report, and even showing dynamic dashboard. 
Classical simulation usually used trial and error approach to find better solution. But it 
takes a lot of cost in perspective of time and less effective.  Researchers have tried to integrate 
optimization technique such as metaheuri stic to be embedded inside simulation software. One 
of famous algorithm that usually used in simulation optimization is Genetic Algorithm (GA) 
[41]. Based on this capability, it can achieve optimal decision in an efficient way. 
 
2.1.8 Flexsim Simulation Software  
Since simulation is one of the main technologies that support digital twin, determining 
simulation software should be considered carefully based on their capability. One of the 
simulation software that provenly can be used as digital twin part is Flexsim software  [42]. 
Flexsim is developed and owned by Flexsim Software Prod ucts, Inc. of Orem, Utah [43]. 
Flexsim is a discrete -event, object -oriented simulator developed in C++, and using Open 
Graphics Library (GL) technology. It has capability to animate the real system through tree 
view, 2 -D, 3 -D, and virtual reality. Many companies use Flexsim to improve production 
efficiencies and reduce operating costs through simulation, experimentation, and optimization 
[44].  
There is a lot of simulation software that supports discrete -event system modeling . 
Compared with other simulation software, research from [45] gives the result that Flexsim is 
the best simulation software in 2018 and 2019 based on criteria Capability, General Purpose, 
Ease of Use, Popularity, and Development. The detailed result is shown in Table 2.2 [45].

Flexsim has so many features that support digital twin concept. In 2017, Flexsim 
introduced new feature to support physical -virtual interconnection called PLC Emulation: 
Emulation tool to control 3D models with simulated programmable logic controllers (PLC ) 
logic. In PLC Emulation, all sensors and PLC data are exchanged with Open Platform 
Communications (OPC) server or directly with PLC through serial communication protocol 
Modbus  [42].  
In Flexsim, PLC Emulation also can be  combine d with optimi zation feature called 
OptQuest implementing Evolutionary Algorithm and SQL databases access that makes 
FlexSim promising choice for prototyping and experimenting with digital twin concept . The 
illustration is shown in Figure 2.8 [42] where digital twin is implemented Shop-floor Service 
System (SSS) with Shop-floor Digital Twin Data (SDTD) . Flexsim also has great 3D 
visualization and customizable dashboard so it will be easier to analyze the real system based 
on Flexsim-based virtual system.  
 
Figure 2. 8 Concept of Digital Twin Supported by Flexsim  
(Source: [42])

2.2 Inductive Review  
The research about DT is done by [10] in realizing Plug-and-Produce fo r Industry 4.0. 
This research uses Asset Administration Shell framework to create DT between 3 Robots & 1 
Turntable as physical assets and uses Web UI as static digital twin. To communicate between 
AAS, OPC UA & AML are used as main technologies. Sequence Diagram is also used to 
represent information flow between AAS. This research focuses on Adaptable Factories (AF) 
as industry 4.0 scenario to be developed.  DT built has capability to control physical assets. 
Different  research [13] utilizes Operator to establish AAS with the help of Smart Jacket 
and create Human-Machine Interface (HMI) as static digital twin and it has capability to control 
physical assets . To communicate between AAS, OPC UA, NodeRED on MCU, and MQQT 
are used as main technologies.  Block Diagram is also used to represent information flow 
between AAS. This research focuses on Operator Support in Production (OSP) as industry 4.0 
scenario to be developed.  Similar research focusing on the human as object of AAS is done by 
[46] that focuses on Operator Support in Production (OSP) as industry 4.0 scenario. Remote 
Control in Forklift Operator is used as physical asset. REST API and MQTT are used as main 
communication tool. GUI was created as static DT but it has no capability to c ontrol physical 
asset. Flowchart is used as information representation.  
Some researchers also have applied AAS in wider area. This research [7] utilizes 
assembly processes  and create Human Machine Interface (HMI) as static digital twin and it has 
capability to control physical assets . OPC UA, CSV, JSON, and REST API  are used as main 
technologies  to communicate between AAS . This research focuses on Adaptable Factories 
(AF) as industry 4.0 scenario to be developed. The information flow between AAS is represent 
by a flowchart . Similar research focusing on DT application in assembly process is done by

3.2. AAS Simulation Modeling 
This research will

propose simulation model as part of dynamic digital twin. Since 
warehouse process can be represented with discrete -event simulation, Flexsim simulation 
software is chosen. In

digital twin, not only functional is important but also visual is crucial. 
Flexsim uses three-dimensional (3D) visualization tha t can imitate operations very similar. In 
this research, Flexsim 2021 version is used. Not all version

s can be used for digital twin 
purpose s because of communication limitation s especially for Flexsim under 2020 version, it 
does not support OPC UA but supports OPC Data Access (DA) and Modbus protocol.

In digital twin modeling, simulation modeling approach will be

different compared with 
classical simulation modeling. It should not be valid but must be verified

structurally and by 
definition . In the other words, it should not imitate the behavior perfectly but must imitate the 
structure of system. Because later on, it will be connected with physical assets in real-time.  
Simulation modeling will follow 5 main stages. First, conceptual modeling. It will 
produce flowchart related to process sequenc

e of smart warehouse operations. Second, layout 
design of smart warehouse. Third, creating some objects/assets needed. This should be the 
same as AAS variables defined  before. Fourth, defining the logic of each asset. Two approaches 
are used in modeling which are standard object modeling to define

general behavior of assets  
and process flow to imitate the specific behavior. The last will be verifying process. The logic 
of simulation must be similar to conce ptual model and must be no error. 
3.3. AAS System Integration 
AAS System Integration consists of designing communication system network based 
on AAS framework  and Information modeling.  AAS framework will develop how assets can 
communicate with other AAS s and what communication devices will be used. On the other 
side, information modeling will be represented by sequence diagram to show how control flow 
and data flow in AAS system . It is created based on RAMI 4.0 layers.  The communication 
protocol and data exchange type will be determined based on the relevance and compliance 
with simulation software used and convention in industry 4.0 community.  
3.4. Demonstration 
Since this research proposes simulation as digital twin especially  using Flexsim 
software, the demonstrations are needed to show the main advantages of using Flexsim  
software in digital twin system. Two demonstrations will be shown  which are simulation-based 
optimization and creating dynamic dashbo ard.  
In classical simulation, What -If analysis usually are used to get better decision. But it 
is less effective  in decision making so that nowadays optimization is integrated with simulation. 
However, if the real condition is too dynamic, the decision ne eds to be taken fast and what-if 
analysis is more appropriate used rather than optimization. The process of simulation -based 
optimization in this research will follow iterative model [58]. Metaheuristic algorithm will give 
input parameters and simulation will use them to be simulated. Then the results will be given 
back to the algorithm. After that, metaheuristic algorithm will improve it and produce new 
input parameters. The process will be iterated until the number of iterations reached. 

24 
 
Metaheurisitic algorithm used in this research is Genetic Algorithm (GA) by following 
OptQuest optimizer [59]. The process of classical GA can be seen in Figure 3. 3 [60]. Since 
optimizer is embedded in Flexsim, all parameters such as population size, crossover rate, and 
mutation rate have been fixed.  There is no elitism strategy used in this original GA. 
 
Figure 3. 3 Genetic Algorithm Process 
(Source: [60]) 
The scenario of s imulation-based optimization in this research will be AGV capacity 
planning. The idea behind it is how many goods should be brought for each AGV so that it will 
maximize throughput  and minimize energy usage simultaneously  under uncertain conditions.  
Since the problem  does not have an analytical closed -form so that it cannot be solved with 
classical optimization technique or mathematical programming such as calculus  or linear 
programming, then simulation is the one to calculate objective functions  under uncertainties. 
The trade-off in this AGV capacity planning problem is the more goods brought in one 
delivery, the more energy usage needed for AGV due to the more weight and the more 
loading/unloading time needed . Since there are 2 objectives, then multi-objective optimization 
is done by using Pareto Optimality approach. This approach will give a set of optimal solutions 
based on Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA-II) and users can choose based 
on their preferences. To  select the single solution, Weighted Sum Method also will be used.

Conveyor will have another sensor in addition to speed sensor which is delivery path 
based on Semantic Product Memory (SemProM). Since warehouse will have some types of 
products, SemProM concept will be adopted to differ product types and object detector w ill 
recognize it. Object detector will be applied in every intersection of conveyor. This information

After define AAS capability , AAS for each asset needs to be defined based on AAS 
structure. Later on, this structure will be applied in Automation Markup Language (AML) when 
the implementation stage is carried on. In this research, AAS template proposed by [49] is used. 
The result of AAS definition is shown  in Table 4. 2. The structure of an AAS can be divided 
into a Digital Factory (DF) header and a DF body  based on the concept of the DF framework 
benchmarked in International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC) standard 62832  [9]. DF 
header describes  a list of I4.0 -compliant propertie s or usually called manifest and DF body 
contains a component manager . 
DF header consists of asset identifier (Asset ID) to differ each type of asset, AAS ID to 
differ every AAS even in the same asset family, for example AGV 1 and AGV 2  will have 
different AAS ID even though they are the same asset , Number of Generic Submodels, and 
Number of Asset-Specific Submodels. Every ID must be unique to ensure that the information 
in submodels can be located appropriately and communicated between AAS in a common 
semantic manner so that interoperability can be realized.  
Generic submodel in DF body means basic standardized submodel applicable to a broad 
variety of assets. Every AAS must include generic submodels to represent mandatory 
information on assets. Index Data Item (IDI) submodel is the entry point of all of the other 
submodels which means that it is essentially a table of contents enabling efficient searching of 
all submodels. Property V alue Statement (PVS) submodel contains metadata that allows users 
to discover the attributes of the asset such as names, versions, and also documentation. Some 
of these properties may change along the asset lifecycle. Hence, this submodel must be 
upgraded regularly. Communication (CM) submodel stores informatio n about communication 
capabilities or communication protocol of the asset. 
 

29 
 
When generic submodel informs static data, contrarily Asset -Specific submodel 
informs dynamic data containing specific information about asset functions. Different assets  
require different submodels to reflect specialized functions. In this manner, considering the 
variety and complexity of each asset function, the number of asset -specific submodels differs. 
For example, AGVs have speed submodel with real value but operator s do not have this 
submodel. Operators will have position submodel with real value because digital model does 
not need to sense the speed of operators but needs to sense the position of operators. This 
determination depends on how the physical assets want to be reflected in digital twin.

4.2. Simulation Model 
Digital twin should be started by detailed simulation modeling. Without a good 
simulation model, digital twin will not reflect physical system well. Before simulation model 
is created, the conceptual model of smart warehouse process should be clear ly described first 
as shown in Figure 4. 2. Layout design is shown in Figure 4. 3. The flow of warehouse 
operations will be described as follows. When goods are arrived by truck, arrival goods will be 
received by forklift and goods will be sent to the Quality Control (QC) process. QC process 
will not generate waste products but it only checks the number and q uality of products. Then, 
storage planning will be done to determine whether the goods will be stored in long -term 
storage or short-term storage. Short-term storage only stores the goods that will be sent in the 
same day, conversely long -term storage only stores the goods that will be sent in another day. 
After storage planning has been determined, Putaway process will be done by forklift either it 
comes from QC process to short -term storage or from long-term storage to short-term storage. 
When the pick-up time arrives, Order picking process is done by AGVs. AGVs will pick goods 
directly in short -term storage in their trajectory. The goods will be sent to shipping stage that 
consists of sorting and packaging process. Work in Process (WIP) from sorting process will be 
stored in the rack temporarily if the packaging process is not finished yet.

4.3. Integration of Digital Twin System 
The proposed concept that wants to be achieved in this research is basically system 
integration to integrate industry 4.0 technologies such as IoT, Simulation, Cloud Computing, 
Big Data Analytics , and others with emphasiz ing of digital twin like shown in Figure 4.6. In 
brief description, data from smart warehouse will be recorded by IoT and will be sent to 
simulation model as digital twin through cloud in real -time. Later on, simulation can be 
extended to be Augmented Real ity (AR) or Virtual Reality (VR). From simulation, command 
or control strategy can be sent to smart warehouse through cloud as well. Transmitted data can 
be stored as big data in the cloud to apply data analytics so that it can  enhance warehousing 
process through Machine Learning (ML), Deep Learning (DL), or Artificial Intelligence (AI). 


34 
 
 
Figure 4. 6 Proposed Concept Development through Digital Twin System  
 
Related to data sensing from assets especially for position data, it will be sensed from 
a transmitter on the top and it will record the tag embedded in movable assets so that the 
movement can be recognized in digital twin as shown in Figure 4. 7. It can be achieved by 
some approaches that already exist nowadays such as Wi -Fi positioning system, RFID long -
range, etc. These data will correspond with Flexsim in position property so that they will 
connect in real -time. For SemProM in storage rack, in order to recognize which cell in rack 
that each product will go, it will corre spond with object detector embedded in every cell as 
shown in Figure 4. 8. These data will correspond with Flexsim in rack property which is level 
(row) or bay (column) so that they can connect in real -time. For SemProM in conveyor, to 
recognize which path  in conveyor that each product will go, it will correspond with object 
detector embedded in every intersection of conveyor like shown in Figure 4. 9. These data will 
correspond with Flexsim in decision point property so that they can connect in real -time.

Digital twin should  not only enable  one-direction flow from physical to digital asset  
(data flow), but it also should enable bi-direction flow from digital to physical asset  (control 
flow). This research allows digital twin to control speed of AGV, capacity of AGV, and speed 
of conveyor as shown in Figure 4. 10. In addition, the most important point is the requirement 
part for simulation software. It should be fulfilled especially for connectivity issues. Flexsim 
allows OPC UA connection based on emulation t ool so that control flow can be done and new 
parameters can be applied with simulated programmable logic controllers (PLC).  
There are two modes in the emulation tool to make a change which are Active and 
Inactive. Direct change should be checked as Active to make a change in real-time. This feature 
allows users to control physical assets directly from digital twin. Otherwise, it will not make a 
change if direct change mode is Inactive. Inactive mode o

ption can be used whenever trial & 
error analysis or opti mization is needed. After optimum parameter is known, then it can be 
applied with Active mode. It is worth noting that not all Flexsim versions can be used for digital 
twin purposes because of communication limitations especially for Flexsim under 2020 version 
that does not support OPC UA communication but it supports OPC DA and Modbus protocol. 
The configuration of sensors and controls also should be clearly defined in Flexsim based on 
AAS Capability  as shown in Figure 4. 11.  Later on, if implementation stage should be done, 
Tag ID must be corresponded with the ID of related AAS and associated with object in Flexsim.

AAS needs system communication network to implement digital twin. Therefore, AAS 
network structure for a smart warehouse including the assets and data exchange paths is shown 
in Figure 4. 12. As

stated before, in this research OPC UA and AML are used as main backbone 
communication between AAS. In simple words, AML will answer what data will be exchanged 
and OPC UA will answer how data are communicated.  
There are 3 reasons why OPC UA and AML  are chosen. First, OPC UA has been 
standardized in IEC 62541  and AML has been standardized in IEC 62714. Second, they are 
compatible with Flexsim software as digital model. Third, AML and OPC UA  adhere  to the 
I4.0 conventions and comply with the RAMI 4.0 model. In AAS system, each as

set has a unique 
AAS ID. However , in terms of simplicity for representation , there are several numbers of the 
same assets like there are 5 AGVs in smart warehouse but it will only show one AGV in Figure 
4. 12 because it will have similar AAS with other AGVs but different ID.  This figure shows 
how AAS connects each other and what communication they use.  
 
Figure 4. 12 AAS Framework for Smart Warehouse  
Take a look at AGV subsystem, there will be speed sensor and speed controller 
connected with AGV through Univers al Asynchronous Receiver–Transmitter (UART). There 
is also position sensor to sense the position of AGV connected through radio waves. AGV itself 
will be connected with Steering Control System through WiFi. Steering Control System has 
capability to control  speed, picking strategy such as AGV capacity, and route.  


39 
 
Steering Control System will be connected with Gateway through OPC UA. There are 
some gateways that can be used. One of them is LM Gateway201-OPC UA protocol. It supports 
wired and wireless protoco ls. The gateway has two functions which are data storage in AML 
and data exchange via OPC UA. From the gateway, it will connect with AAS of Flexsim 
through OPC UA and Flexsim will show real-time condition through external interface . 
The interaction between  AAS is shown as a sequence diagram in Figure 4. 1 3 that 
represents the dynamic information flow and control flow across the digitalized network of 
AASs by following RAMI 4.0 layers. First, the AML-related OPC UA server will be initialized. 
Then, OPC UA sessions will be created with different clients which are O PC UA

Client from 
AAS of Flexsim and OPC UA Interface from AAS of Field devices. Control flows are then 
generated by the users from AAS Flexsim and passed down to the AASs of the field devices. 
Here, warehousing tasks are executed according to the control strategy. Feedback parameters 
are then given back to the AAS of Flexsim. Thus, the digital twin system operates in a loop.

4.4.1 Simulation-based Optimization for AGV Capacity Planning 
AGV takes crucial part in smart warehouse  operation. It brings goods from storage to 
the shipping

area and it has their own trajectory. Capacity of AGV determines total throughput 
and energy usage since AGV uses electricity energy in their operation. However, the more 
goods carried out, the heavier they are that takes longer loading & unloading time and the more 
energy usage needed. Therefore, ther

e is trade -off in this problem. Even tough there are some 
objectives also that can be considered such as optimizing the route of AGV and the utilization 
of worker in warehouse, but only total throughput and energy usage will be considered here 
since tho

se objectives influence the customer satisfaction, profit, and operation cost directly.  
To solve Multi-Objective Optimization (MOO), GA based on NSGA-II is used. 
Simulation brings more advanced optimization by considering uncert ain conditions such as 
goods availability and process time . Simulation is run for 1 day operation or 7 hours or 25200 
seconds working time.  It is assumed that every load of goods brought by AGV will take 2 
ampere per meter and if there are no goods brought it will only take 1 ampere per meter. There 
is also no charging condition considered . The optimization code used is shown in Figure 1 4. 
The results for GA searching within 100 iterations are shown in Figure 4.  15 for throughput 
(unit) and Figure 4. 16 for energy consumption in ampere (amp).  The purple circle shows the 
suggested candidate solution. The set of non-dominated solutions is shown in Table 4. 4.

CONCLUSION AND RECOMMENDATION 
5.1 Conclusion 
The new concept of simulation -based digital twin based on Asset Administration Shell 
(AAS) framework  has been proposed by using Flexsim software with  smart warehouse as a 
use-case. Asset-specific submodels  also have been proposed for Automated Guided Vehicle 
(AGV), Operator, Conveyor, Forklift, and Storage Rack. AAS capability also has been 
designed which digital twin is able to monitor and control the real system as well as providing 
optimization . It needs OPC UA and AutomationML as c ommunication protocol and data 
exchange type between AAS.  
The verified simulation model imitating smart warehouse has been constructed as well 
built in  Flexsim software. It has imitated the main assets in smart warehouse as well as the 
layout and logic behind it. System integration architecture also has been proposed through AAS 
framework and sequence diagram to help the understanding on how data and control flow from 
all AASs including AAS of Flexsim as digital twin. 
Since this research prop

oses simulation with Flexsim software as digital twin, two main 
advantages have been demonstrated. The result of simulation -based optimization for multi -
objective AGV capacity planning shows that in order to maximize the total throughput  and 
minimize energy usage simultaneously, solution ID 95 gives optimum result where the capacity 
for each AGV is as follows: AGV 1 (X1) = 9, AGV 2 (X2) = 9, AGV 3 (X3) = 9, AGV 4 (X4) 
= 8, AGV 5 (X5) = 9. This is going to be breakthrough for Digital Twin research since previous 
state-of-the-art analysis about DT stated that even there are still lack of DT that can reflect 
optimization.  The result of designing dynamic dashboard also has been demonstrated  to 
increase the capability and accuracy of controlling and monitoring such as total throughput for 
each product type, AGV Utilization , and Operator State. 
 
 
 
 

47 
 
5.2 Recommendation 
Recommendation will be given for two st

akeholders, namely smart warehouse and further work 
as follows: 
1. Recommendation for smart warehouse 
Smart warehouse has not only a lot of assets but also movable assets that need to be 
analyzed carefully. This is because warehouse is an important entity in logistics. In order 
to enhance smart warehouse operation,  system

integration through digital twin 
establishment based on AAS framework is very appropriate to be implemented , this 
research  is a starting point to implement digital twin as the basic technology for system 
integration. Two-stage DT application  has been done in this research  which are system 
design of AAS and simulation modeling. Other warehouses also can use this proposed 
concept as well as methodology for establishing simulation -based digital twin that has 
been constructed

in this research and start to apply 2 next stages which are hardware 
installation and PLC programming to connect digital assets and physical assets based on 
t

he proposed communication protocol . 
2. Recommendation for further work  
In reality, there are still many lacks of industry 4.0 scenario application  especially in 
system integration. Moreover, there is still a few researches working on AAS as the key 
of integration and standardization. Since this research contributes in proposing simulation 
model especially Flexsim software as dynamic digital twin and have tried to consider 
assets a wider area, further work can start to research in the implementation stage and find 
efficient  way. Economic analysis also should be considered in order to achieve minimum 
cost in implementing digital twin. Until today, it is still hard to establish standard manner 
to deploy digital twin without AAS. Later on , a lot of industries can not be integrated. 
Without standardization, each industry will have its own digital twin and each industry 
will stand by itself without any integration and communication between them. That is not 
what industry 4.0 targeted . Moreover, this proposed concept also can be integrated with 
data analytics by using the same AAS framework with some additional s ubmodels. Some 
of data will be sent to the simula

tion that will be used as monitoring and controlling based 
on optimized decision and others will be stored in the cloud as big data to establish 
predictive maintenance and give the control to the physical as set automatically. Digital 
twin from simulation also can be extended into AR or VR to enhance its capability. Thus, 
industry 4.0 scenarios can be integrated systematically and comprehensively.

Demonstration Since this research proposes simulation as digital twin especially using Flexsim software, the demonstrations are needed to show the main advantages of using Flexsim software in digital twin system. Two demonstrations will be shown which are simulation-based optimization and creating dynamic dashbo ard.

In classical simulation, What -If analysis usually are used to get better decision. But it is less effective in decision making so that nowadays optimization is integrated with simulation. However, if the real condition is too dynamic, the decision ne eds to be taken fast and what-if analysis is more appropriate used rather than optimization. The process of simulation -based optimization in this research will follow iterative model [58]. Metaheuristic algorithm will give input parameters and simulation will use them to be simulated.

Then the results will be given back to the algorithm. After that, metaheuristic algorithm will improve it and produce new input parameters. The process will be iterated until the number of iterations reached. 24 Metaheurisitic algorithm used in this research is Genetic Algorithm (GA) by following OptQuest optimizer [59]. The process of classical GA can be seen in Figure 3. 3 [60]. Since optimizer is embedded in Flexsim, all parameters such as population size, crossover rate, and mutation rate have been fixed. There is no elitism strategy used in this original GA. Figure 3.

3 Genetic Algorithm Process (Source: [60]) The scenario of s imulation-based optimization in this research will be AGV capacity planning. The idea behind it is how many goods should be brought for each AGV so that it will maximize throughput and minimize energy usage simultaneously under uncertain conditions. Since the problem does not have an analytical closed -form so that it cannot be solved with classical optimization technique or mathematical programming such as calculus or linear programming, then simulation is the one to calculate objective functions under uncertainties. The trade-off in this AGV capacity planning problem is the more goods brought in one delivery, the more energy usage needed for AGV due to the more weight and the more loading/unloading time needed . Since there are 2 objectives, then multi-objective optimization is done by using Pareto Optimality approach. This approach will give a set of optimal solutions based on Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA-II) and users can choose based on their preferences. To select the single solution, Weighted Sum Method also will be used. In this research, AAS template proposed by [49] is used. The result of AAS definition is shown in Table 4. 2.

The structure of an AAS can be divided into a Digital Factory (DF) header and a DF body based on the concept of the DF framework benchmarked in International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC) standard 62832 [9]. DF header describes a list of I4.0 -compliant propertie s or usually called manifest and DF body contains a component manager .

DF header consists of asset identifier (Asset ID) to differ each type of asset, AAS ID to differ every AAS even in the same asset family, for example AGV 1 and AGV 2 will have different AAS ID even though they are the same asset , Number of Generic Submodels, and Number of Asset-Specific Submodels. Every ID must be unique to ensure that the information in submodels can be located appropriately and communicated between AAS in a common semantic manner so that interoperability can be realized.
Generic submodel in DF body means basic standardized submodel applicable to a broad variety of assets. Every AAS must include generic submodels to represent mandatory information on assets.

Index Data Item (IDI) submodel is the entry point of all of the other submodels which means that it is essentially a table of contents enabling efficient searching of all submodels. Property V alue Statement (PVS) submodel contains metadata that allows users to discover the attributes of the asset such as names, versions, and also documentation. Some of these properties may change along the asset lifecycle. Hence, this submodel must be upgraded regularly. Communication (CM) submodel stores informatio n about communication capabilities or communication protocol of the asset.

29 When generic submodel informs static data, contrarily Asset -Specific submodel informs dynamic data containing specific information about asset functions. Different assets require different submodels to reflect specialized functions. In this manner, considering the variety and complexity of each asset function, the number of asset -specific submodels differs.

For example, AGVs have speed submodel with real value but operator s do not have this submodel. Operators will have position submodel with real value because digital model does not need to sense the speed of operators but needs to sense the position of operators. Simulation Model Digital twin should be started by detailed simulation modeling. Without a good simulation model, digital twin will not reflect physical system well. Before simulation model is created, the conceptual model of smart warehouse process should be clear ly described first as shown in Figure 4. 2. Layout design is shown in Figure 4. 3.

The flow of warehouse operations will be described as follows. When goods are arrived by truck, arrival goods will be received by forklift and goods will be sent to the Quality Control (QC) process. QC process will not generate waste products but it only checks the number and q uality of products.

Then, storage planning will be done to determine whether the goods will be stored in long -term storage or short-term storage. Short-term storage only stores the goods that will be sent in the same day, conversely long -term storage only stores the goods that will be sent in another day. After storage planning has been determined, Putaway process will be done by forklift either it comes from QC process to short -term storage or from long-term storage to short-term storage.
When the pick-up time arrives, Order picking process is done by AGVs. AGVs will pick goods directly in short -term storage in their trajectory.

The goods will be sent to shipping stage that consists of sorting and packaging process. Work in Process (WIP) from sorting process will be stored in the rack temporarily if the packaging process is not finished yet. Figure 4.

As stated before, in this research OPC UA and AML are used as main backbone communication between AAS. The interaction between AAS is shown as a sequence diagram in Figure 4. 1 3 that represents the dynamic information flow and control flow across the digitalized network of AASs by following RAMI 4.0 layers.

First, the AML-related OPC UA server will be initialized. Then, OPC UA sessions will be created with different clients which are O PC UA Client from AAS of Flexsim and OPC UA Interface from AAS of Field devices. Control flows are then generated by the users from AAS Flexsim and passed down to the AASs of the field devices. Here, warehousing tasks are executed according to the control strategy. Feedback parameters are then given back to the AAS of Flexsim. Thus, the digital twin system operates in a loop. Figure 4. 13 Interaction of AAS 4.4. It brings goods from storage to the shipping area and it has their own trajectory.

Capacity of AGV determines total throughput and energy usage since AGV uses electricity energy in their operation. However, the more goods carried out, the heavier they are that takes longer loading & unloading time and the more energy usage needed. Therefore, there is trade -off in this problem. Even tough there are some objectives also that can be considered such as optimizing the route of AGV and the utilization of worker in warehouse, but only total throughput and energy usage will be considered here since those objectives influence the customer satisfaction, profit, and operation cost directly.

Simulation brings more advanced optimization by considering uncert ain conditions such as goods availability and process time . Simulation is run for 1 day operation or 7 hours or 25200 seconds working time. It is assumed that every load of goods brought by AGV will take 2 ampere per meter and if there are no goods brought it will only take 1 ampere per meter. There is also no charging condition considered .

From AGV utilization, optimized result also gives higher percentage of traveling but lower percentage of loading/unloading. However longer traveling does not mean higher energy usage, but it depends also with how many loads that AGV brings per travel. By using this kind of flexible dashboard, it will ease users to take decision in order to optimize warehouse operation under uncertainty based on simulation-based digital twin. The more interesting one is, this proposed concept can implement the optimum decision directly from the software to the shopfloor without changing it from shopfloor manually. CHAPTER 5 CONCLUSION AND RECOMMENDATION 5.1 Conclusion The new concept of simulation -based digital twin based on Asset Administration Shell (AAS) framework has been proposed by using Flexsim software with smart warehouse as a use-case. Asset-specific submodels also have been proposed for Automated Guided Vehicle (AGV), Operator, Conveyor, Forklift, and Storage Rack.

AAS capability also has been designed which digital twin is able to monitor and control the real system as well as providing optimization . It needs OPC UA and AutomationML as c ommunication protocol and data exchange type between AAS.

The verified simulation model imitating smart warehouse has been constructed as well built in Flexsim software. It has imitated the main assets in smart warehouse as well as the layout and logic behind it.

System integration architecture also has been proposed through AAS framework and sequence diagram to help the understanding on how data and control flow from all AASs including AAS of Flexsim as digital twin. Since this research proposes simulation with Flexsim software as digital twin, two main advantages have been demonstrated. The result of simulation -based optimization for multi - objective AGV capacity planning shows that in order to maximize the total throughput and minimize energy usage simultaneously, solution ID 95 gives optimum result where the capacity for each AGV is as follows: AGV 1 (X1) = 9, AGV 2 (X2) = 9, AGV 3 (X3) = 9, AGV 4 (X4) = 8, AGV 5 (X5) = 9. This is going to be breakthrough for Digital Twin research since previous state-of-the-art analysis about DT stated that even there are still lack of DT that can reflect optimization. The result of designing dynamic dashboard also has been demonstrated to increase the capability and accuracy of controlling and monitoring such as total throughput for each product type, AGV Utilization , and Operator State.

47 5.2 Recommendation Recommendation will be given for two stakeholders, namely smart warehouse and further work as follows: 1. Recommendation for smart warehouse Smart warehouse has not only a lot of assets but also movable assets that need to be analyzed carefully. This is because warehouse is an important entity in logistics.

In order to enhance smart warehouse operation, system integration through digital twin establishment based on AAS framework is very appropriate to be implemented , this research is a starting point to implement digital twin as the basic technology for system integration. Two-stage DT application has been done in this research which are system design of AAS and simulation modeling. Other warehouses also can use this proposed concept as well as methodology for establishing simulation -based digital twin that has been constructed in this research and start to apply 2 next stages which are hardware installation and PLC programming to connect digital assets and physical assets based on the proposed communication protocol . 2. Recommendation for further work In reality, there are still many lacks of industry 4.0 scenario application especially in system integration. Moreover, there is still a few researches working on AAS as the key of integration and standardization.

Since this research contributes in proposing simulation model especially Flexsim software as dynamic digital twin and have tried to consider assets a wider area, further work can start to research in the implementation stage and find efficient way. Economic analysis also should be considered in order to achieve minimum cost in implementing digital twin. Until today, it is still hard to establish standard manner to deploy digital twin without AAS.

Later on , a lot of industries can not be integrated. Without standardization, each industry will have its own digital twin and each industry will stand by itself without any integration and communication between them. That is not what industry 4.0 targeted . Moreover, this proposed concept also can be integrated with data analytics by using the same AAS framework with some additional s ubmodels. Some of data will be sent to the simulation that will be used as monitoring and controlling based on optimized decision and others will be stored in the cloud as big data to establish predictive maintenance and give the control to the physical as set automatically. Digital twin from simulation also can be extended into AR or VR to enhance its capability.
